compiled_state.author: Joe Oakhart

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body:
Plan-review advisory dispositions (17 findings, review @5a39f85eb PASS):

ACCEPTED (reflected in plan/tests/impl):
- #1 & #4 (0.54, dup): fsck_recover gate must NOT block --detect-only (read-only diagnostic). ACCEPT — gate only the mutating path (skip when detect_only). Reflected: held-out test invokes fsck-recover WITHOUT --detect-only; impl skips gate for detect-only.
- #3 (0.54): init/MCP-boot entry points need StoreIncompatibleError->non-zero conversion, not just init.py ensure unit. ACCEPT — already added via the run_ensures re-raise remediation (brief §6b); init/mcp-boot surface it as non-zero exit.
- #5 (0.26): describe_store_compat -> dict|None named export for fsck diagnostic/AC6. ACCEPT — defined as {kind,detail} dict in remediation.
- #7 (0.26): jq -e '.compat_error.kind' matches any string. ACCEPT — held-out test asserts exact value (.compat_error.kind == "unknown_format_version"), stronger than presence.
- #9 (0.26): more write_lock() callers exist than enumerated. ACCEPT (informational) — the single gate inside lock.acquire() covers ALL of them by construction; coverage-lint test greps for competing acquire()/write_lock() definitions.
- #10 (0.0): _version.py format_version/SCHEMA_VERSION note. ACCEPT — comment-only clarification.
- #11 (0.0): run_ensures "Never raises" docstring now inaccurate. ACCEPT — update docstring to note it raises StoreIncompatibleError (fail-closed).

REJECTED (with rationale):
- #2 (0.4): vague "AC/DoD quality" — REJECT, ACs are already concrete + test-backed.
- #6 (0.26): git-stash vs monkeypatch rollback-proof mismatch — REJECT, rollback safety is that a pre-v1.0 binary ignores the unknown file; documented in docs/migrations.md, no code change warranted.
- #8 (0.26): what is logged on a healthy no-op check — REJECT, a compatible/absent record is a silent no-op by design.
- #12 (0.0): TOCTOU (record read before fcntl) — REJECT, the record is written only by the ensure unit under the same lock and is effectively immutable at runtime; a fail-closed safety check, not a serialization point; the next write re-checks.
- #13 (0.0): no AC for format_version/SCHEMA_VERSION relationship — REJECT, covered by the _version.py comment.
- #14 (0.0): permission-denied corrupt sub-case untested — REJECT the test (chmod-based perm tests are flaky / root-bypassed) but ACCEPT the code path: check_store_compat maps OSError -> StoreIncompatibleError.
- #15 & #16 (0.0): reconcile.py:133/670 citation is a param line, not the tracker path — REJECT as a citation nitpick; implementation uses repo_root/".tickets-tracker" correctly regardless.
- #17 (0.0): docs/releasing.md omitted — REJECT for this leaf; the store-compat record is a migrations concern (docs/migrations.md); releasing.md is owned by the release stories F1/F3/F4.

compiled_state.description:
# Committed store compatibility record with a fail-closed capability gate

Introduce a committed store-level compatibility record (`format_version` +
`required_capabilities`) that a v1.0 binary reads before any mutating or
externally-publishing operation, failing closed on an unknown version or required
capability while keeping reads and diagnostics available. (Readiness Finding 2 —
verified VALID against `src/rebar/_store/ensures.py`, `src/rebar/reducer/_version.py`,
`src/rebar/_commands/{compact,fsck,init}.py`, `docs/migrations.md`.)

## Why

rebar's migration mechanism is an idempotent desired-state ensure-registry, not an
ordered ledger: `run_ensures` runs every unit under the write lock, converts
exceptions to `failed`, and **returns normally** (`src/rebar/_store/ensures.py`
docstring "Never raises"; init/boot never abort). Event compatibility is
preserve-and-ignore: unknown event types are skipped in replay
(`reducer/_replay.py:195-196`) and omitted from the compaction fold but kept on
disk (`_commands/compact.py:179-195`), and `fsck` emits an informational WARN that
does not increment `issue_count` or stop operation (`_commands/fsck.py:229-252`,
which itself notes "a reconcile host on an old binary may push stale state").

This forward-compat is correct for *optional additive* data — it prevents an old
compactor from deleting new bytes. But there is **no committed store-format-version
or required-capability record** (confirmed absent; `SCHEMA_VERSION` is a stamp,
never compared to fail closed). So an old binary computes state that omits the
unknown event and can then emit a new event, compact, evaluate close/verify policy,
or push a stale field to Jira from that incomplete state. `docs/migrations.md`
explicitly files the "A-tier ledger + committed store-format version that fails
closed" under future work. **The reader must ship in v1.0**: an already-installed
v1.0 binary cannot be taught later to inspect a record whose existence and
semantics were not defined in v1.0 — so to ever mark a store as requiring a
post-v1.0 capability, v1.0 must already know how to detect and refuse unknown
required capabilities.

## What

Adopt git's repository-format-version pattern
(`core.repositoryformatversion` + `extensions.*`: an implementation that does not
understand the advertised format or a required extension must not operate, because
continuing can produce incorrect results or lose data), and git's staged rollout
(teach readers first, make writing opt-in, default later):

- **The record.** Add a committed store-level record, minimally
  `{"format_version": 1, "required_capabilities": []}`, at a fixed store path (e.g.
  `.store-compat.json`). It is **committed to the `tickets` branch** (not
  git-ignored) — it is the shared fact the gate reads. Write it with the store's
  standard write-to-tempfile-then-atomic-rename pattern so a partial/interrupted
  write never leaves a truncated file.
- **Recognised-capability vocabulary.** The new module exports
  `KNOWN_CAPABILITIES: frozenset[str] = frozenset()` — the set of capability strings
  **this** binary understands. The gate blocks when any entry in a record's
  `required_capabilities` is not in `KNOWN_CAPABILITIES`. Future capabilities are
  added to this set only when the feature that consumes them ships (git
  `extensions.*` semantics).
- **The gate error.** The module exports `StoreIncompatibleError(Exception)`, raised
  by the gate; entry-point callers convert it to the existing `CommandError` /
  non-zero exit path (as other gate errors do), with a message naming the
  unsupported version/capability.
- **Four record states (explicit).** (1) **Absent** → implicit legacy format
  version 0, compatible / pass-through, NOT blocked. (2) **Present + compatible** →
  pass. (3) **Present + incompatible** (unknown `format_version` or a
  `required_capabilities` entry ∉ `KNOWN_CAPABILITIES`) → **fail-closed**. (4)
  **Present + unreadable/corrupt** (JSON parse error, truncation, permission denied)
  → **fail-closed** with a diagnostic message naming the parse error and the record
  path (a corrupt record is NOT treated as absent — that would silently bypass the
  gate).
- **Expand phase (writing the record into pre-v1.0 stores).** Every store created by
  a pre-v1.0 build is state (1). An idempotent ensure unit (School B) writes the
  record at the current version when absent, at init / fsck-repair / MCP-boot.
  **Because `run_ensures` is fail-silent** (a `LockTimeout` silently skips the whole
  sweep; exceptions become `failed` and it returns normally), the covered-write path
  must not *depend* on the ensure sweep having run: the gate treats absent as
  pass-through regardless, and the record is written opportunistically. So a missed
  sweep never blocks writes — it only defers stamping the record to a later covered
  operation. **absent = legacy/needs-migration, not blocked; present-incompatible or
  corrupt = blocked.**
- **Gate at the true structural choke point, NOT by enumerating call-sites.** "No
  mutation without the gate" must not rest on a hand-maintained list a future path can
  bypass. The single convergence point for **all lock-based store mutations** is
  **`lock.acquire()` in `src/rebar/_store/lock.py`** — both the `write_lock()` context
  manager AND the direct callers that bypass it (`txn.py`'s `_acquire_write_lock` for
  claim/transition, `compact.py:167`/`:415`, `fsck.py:419`) call `acquire()`. Insert
  the `check_store_compat()` gate **inside `lock.acquire()`**, after `canonical_tracker()`
  resolves the path and before the fcntl/mkdir legs — so every lock-holding path
  (context-manager and raw-handle) is covered by one insertion point, with no
  enumeration. **Gate ALL `lock.acquire()` unconditionally — no `read_only` parameter
  needed.** The diagnostic exemption is already **structural**: `fsck`'s diagnostic
  path reads ticket data **without acquiring the lock at all**, so it is inherently
  ungated and stays available; only `fsck`'s **repair** path (which *does* call
  `lock.acquire()` at `fsck.py:419`), and `compact.py`/`txn.py`, hold the lock and are
  therefore gated. This is simpler and less error-prone than a read/write-intent flag.
- **`run_ensures` must NOT swallow the gate (fail-closed integrity).** `run_ensures`
  (`src/rebar/_store/ensures.py`) wraps its whole `with write_lock(...)` body in a broad
  `except Exception` that logs-and-returns (honouring its "Never raises" sweep contract).
  Because the gate raises `StoreIncompatibleError` **inside `lock.acquire()`** — which
  `write_lock()` calls — that broad handler would **catch and swallow it**, turning a
  fail-closed incompatibility into a silent sweep no-op for every caller that self-heals
  through `run_ensures` (init, MCP-boot's ensure sweep, `fsck --repair`). Remediate by
  adding an explicit `except StoreIncompatibleError: raise` **before** the broad
  `except Exception` in `run_ensures`, so the incompatibility propagates to the caller;
  the init / MCP-boot / fsck-repair entry points then surface it as a **non-zero exit /
  refused startup** (converting `StoreIncompatibleError` to their existing
  `CommandError` / error path). This keeps the gate un-swallowable on the ensure-sweep
  path. (Note: `StoreIncompatibleError` subclasses `Exception`, so this explicit re-raise
  is required — it is not skipped automatically the way a `BaseException` would be.)
- **Two paths do NOT go through `lock.acquire()` and each gets its own explicit gate:**
  1. **`fsck_recover.py`** performs raw `git cherry-pick`/`rebase --continue`/`merge
     --abort` via its `_git()` helper with **no locking at all** — so it is genuinely
     un-gated by the lock choke point. Add an explicit `check_store_compat(tracker_dir)`
     at the top of `fsck_recover_cli` before any mutation. (Wrapping the mutation block
     in `write_lock` too would additionally close a real pre-existing concurrency gap,
     but at minimum the explicit gate is required.)
  2. **Outbound Jira publish** — the push/reconcile side effect is a **remote API
     call**, not a lock-held write. Jira writes are scattered
     (`create_issue`/`update_issue`/`delete_issue`/`add_comment` across
     `apply_outbound.py` + `dispatch_one.py`), but they all funnel once per pass
     through **`reconcile._apply_mutations()`** (`src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/reconcile.py`,
     which calls `applier.apply(...)`). **Exact insertion point (no top-level `persist`
     guard exists in this function):** `_apply_mutations()` binds `persist = ctx.persist`
     near the top and threads it down into `applier.apply(...)`; it has **no**
     `if persist:` / `if not persist: return` branch of its own. So add the gate
     explicitly: immediately after `persist = ctx.persist` is bound (before the
     filter-narrowing / preflight block), insert
     `if persist: check_store_compat(repo_root / ".tickets-tracker")`. Because dry-run /
     reconcile-check / cap-0 passes run with `persist=False`, the `if persist:` guard
     excludes them; live (persisting) passes are gated before any `applier.apply(...)`
     Jira write. This mirrors the existing fail-closed cross-project guard precedent at
     `applier.py:637-652` ("refuse BEFORE issuing any Jira write … abort the whole pass,
     no partial writes"). **Use the reconciler's own tracker path**
     (`repo_root / ".tickets-tracker"`, hardcoded at `reconcile.py:133/670`), NOT
     `config.tracker_dir()`, since the reconciler resolves the store directly.
- **Completeness is CI-enforced.** A coverage test/lint greps the tree for **both**
  `lock.acquire(` and `write_lock(` (and asserts each routes through the gate) plus
  asserts `fsck_recover_cli` calls `check_store_compat` — so a new un-gated write path,
  in any of the acquisition forms, fails CI rather than relying on this prose list.
- **Read/diagnostic allowance + the structured report shape (defined).** Under an
  incompatible/corrupt record, the read-only commands — `fsck` (diagnostic mode),
  `show`, `list`, `search` — stay available (exit 0). The incompatibility is surfaced in
  a **defined, machine-checkable shape**: `fsck`'s diagnostic JSON gains a top-level
  `compat_error` object
  `{"compat_error": {"kind": "unknown_format_version" | "unknown_capability" | "corrupt_record",
  "detail": "<human string naming the offending version / capability / parse-error + the
  record path>"}}` (and the same message on a stderr `WARNING` line). The `compat` module
  exports a **non-raising** `describe_store_compat(tracker) -> dict | None` that returns
  exactly this `{"kind", "detail"}` payload (or `None` when compatible/absent); the fsck
  diagnostic path calls it and, when non-`None`, attaches it as `compat_error` and emits
  the WARNING — without changing the exit code. AC6 asserts `.compat_error.kind` via
  `jq -e` so the check is deterministic. `fsck`'s **repair** mode (write-intent) is gated
  like any other mutation (non-zero exit).
- **Rollback safety (decided, then proven).** Post-rollback the committed
  `.store-compat.json` remains on the `tickets` branch unchanged, and a pre-v1.0 binary
  **ignores it** (ignored-unknown → rollback-safe): `.store-compat.json` is a *new*
  file at a path no pre-v1.0 code reads — pre-v1.0 replay/compaction/fsck open only
  event files and the known metadata files (`.env-id`, `.gitignore`, etc.), never this
  name — so there is no parse-and-error path. This is the committed design position
  (not a deferral), and the **hard rollback test below is its empirical proof** (git-
  stash the gate code, run `rebar fsck` against a store containing `.store-compat.json`,
  assert operable + file byte-unchanged). The delete-`.store-compat.json` recovery is
  documented as belt-and-suspenders only.
- **Scope boundary.** Keep the ensure-registry for convergent check-then-act repairs.
  Do **not** build the ordered ledger here — only the record + reader gate. Clarify
  the relationship to `SCHEMA_VERSION` in `reducer/_version.py` (it stays the
  event-schema discriminator; `format_version` is the new store-format gate — state
  whether they move together or independently).

## Scope

- New module under `src/rebar/_store/` for the compatibility record + gate, exporting
  `StoreIncompatibleError`, `KNOWN_CAPABILITIES`, and `check_store_compat()`.
- `src/rebar/_store/lock.py` — insert the gate inside `lock.acquire()` (the single
  convergence point for `write_lock()` + all direct `lock.acquire()` callers:
  `txn.py`, `compact.py:167`/`:415`, `fsck.py:419`), gating every acquisition
  unconditionally (fsck diagnostic holds no lock → structurally exempt).
- `src/rebar/_commands/fsck_recover.py` — explicit `check_store_compat()` before its
  raw-git mutation (it holds no lock, so the `lock.acquire()` gate does not cover it).
- `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/reconcile.py` — `if persist: check_store_compat(repo_root/".tickets-tracker")`
  inserted in `_apply_mutations()` immediately after `persist = ctx.persist` is bound
  (the function has no top-level `persist` guard; the `if persist:` wrapper is the single
  per-pass chokepoint for both write paths and excludes dry-run/cap-0 passes).
- `src/rebar/_store/ensures.py` — add `except StoreIncompatibleError: raise` before
  `run_ensures`'s broad `except Exception` so the gate is not swallowed on the ensure
  sweep; add a `store-compat` ensure unit id.
- A coverage test/lint grepping `lock.acquire(` + `write_lock(` + `fsck_recover`'s check.
- `src/rebar/reducer/_version.py` — relate `format_version` to the existing
  preserve-and-ignore `KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES` machinery and to `SCHEMA_VERSION`
  (optional-additive events stay preserve-and-ignore; a required capability is the
  new fail-closed path).
- `src/rebar/_commands/init.py` — the ensure unit that writes the record when absent.
- `docs/migrations.md` — document the record, the four states, the fail-closed
  semantics, the absent-record upgrade path, and the concrete rollback state.
- Interface tests extending `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_event_schema_forward_compat.py`.
- Out of scope: the ordered migration ledger; changing any existing event-replay
  behaviour for currently-known types.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] A committed store compatibility record (`format_version` +
  `required_capabilities`) is created at init at a fixed path, written atomically
  (tempfile + rename), and read by a gate function that raises
  `StoreIncompatibleError`; the module exports `KNOWN_CAPABILITIES: frozenset[str]`.
- [ ] An **absent** record (pre-v1.0 store) is treated as legacy format version 0 /
  compatible (pass-through, not blocked), and the ensure unit idempotently writes the
  record on the next init / fsck-repair / MCP-boot — verified by a test that opens a
  record-less store with a v1.0 build, confirms it operates, and confirms the record
  is then present (upgrade-in-place, no manual step); the test also confirms a missed
  ensure sweep does not block writes — by monkeypatching `run_ensures` to raise
  `LockTimeout` (or return a `failed` outcome) and asserting a subsequent write still
  succeeds (the gate treats the still-absent record as pass-through).
- [ ] The gate is inserted inside `lock.acquire()` (`src/rebar/_store/lock.py`, after
  path resolution, before fcntl/mkdir) and gates **every** acquisition unconditionally
  — `write_lock()` context-manager callers AND direct `lock.acquire()` callers
  (`txn.py`, `compact.py`, `fsck.py:419` repair) — by one insertion point; `fsck`
  diagnostic holds no lock so it is structurally exempt. Plus an explicit
  `check_store_compat()` at the top of `fsck_recover_cli` (raw-git, no-lock mutation)
  and in `reconcile._apply_mutations()` as `if persist: check_store_compat(repo_root/".tickets-tracker")`
  inserted immediately after `persist = ctx.persist` is bound (there is no top-level
  `persist` guard in that function; the `if persist:` wrapper is what excludes
  dry-run/reconcile-check/cap-0 passes), before `applier.apply()`, for the outbound
  Jira push. It raises `StoreIncompatibleError` (→ non-zero exit) before any side
  effect when the record is **present but incompatible**. Verified by (a) a test in
  `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_event_schema_forward_compat.py` writing a record
  with a `required_capabilities` entry ∉ `KNOWN_CAPABILITIES` (e.g. `["unknown-v99"]`)
  and driving a lock-held mutation (store files byte-unchanged), an `fsck_recover`
  invocation, and a reconcile pass (mock Jira adapter records zero calls); and (b) a
  coverage test/lint that greps the tree for **both** `lock.acquire(` and `write_lock(`
  and for `fsck_recover_cli`'s + `_apply_mutations`'s `check_store_compat` call,
  asserting each routes through the
  gate — so a new un-gated write path in any acquisition form fails CI.
- [ ] A **corrupt/unreadable** record (truncated JSON / parse error) is treated as
  fail-closed (NOT as absent) with a diagnostic naming the parse error and path —
  verified by a test writing a truncated record and asserting a mutating entry point
  is blocked.
- [ ] A rollback **test** (hard requirement) simulates a pre-v1.0 binary by invoking
  the store layer with the gate patched out (monkeypatch `check_store_compat` to a
  no-op, emulating code that predates it) against a store carrying the committed
  `.store-compat.json`, and asserts **operable** = `rebar list`/`fsck` exit 0 AND a
  normal write (e.g. `comment`) succeeds, and the `.store-compat.json` file is
  byte-unchanged afterward (proving upgrade-in-place won't brick a pre-v1.0 clone). The
  ignored-unknown guarantee and the delete-`.store-compat.json` compensating path are
  also documented in `docs/migrations.md`.
- [ ] Under an incompatible/corrupt record, the read-only commands `fsck` (diagnostic
  mode), `show`, `list`, and `search` exit with the read-allowed code (0) and `fsck`'s
  diagnostic JSON carries a defined `compat_error` object
  `{"compat_error": {"kind": "unknown_format_version"|"unknown_capability"|"corrupt_record",
  "detail": "<...>"}}` (produced by the non-raising `describe_store_compat()`), with the
  same message on a stderr `WARNING` line — while `fsck` **repair** mode (write-intent)
  exits non-zero — verified by a test asserting `.compat_error.kind` via `jq -e` (a
  deterministic field check, not a substring grep) and the repair-mode non-zero exit.
- [ ] The gate is **un-swallowable on the ensure-sweep path**: `run_ensures`'s broad
  `except Exception` does not silently absorb `StoreIncompatibleError` (it has an explicit
  `except StoreIncompatibleError: raise` before it), so init / MCP-boot / `fsck --repair`
  against a present-but-incompatible store fail closed (non-zero exit / refused startup)
  rather than logging a sweep no-op — verified by a test that stamps an incompatible
  record and asserts `run_ensures` (or the init entry point that calls it) surfaces the
  error rather than returning a benign `failed` outcome.
- [ ] Optional additive (unknown-but-not-*required*) events retain today's
  preserve-and-ignore behaviour — the existing forward-compat contract tests still
  pass, and a new test proves a *required* capability fails closed while an *optional*
  additive event does not.
- [ ] `docs/migrations.md` documents the record, the four states, the fail-closed
  gate, the deployment-order rationale (why the reader ships in v1.0), and the
  concrete rollback state.
- [ ] `make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass, and the change lands via the
  Gerrit two-vote gate.
compiled_state.title: Committed store compatibility record with a fail-closed capability gate
